figures.name: Modu Chanyu
figures.significance: United the steppe tribes; template for all subsequent steppe empires

figures.name: Bumin Qaghan
figures.significance: First use of the title Khagan; founded the first explicitly Turkic empire

figures.name: Bilge Qaghan
figures.significance: Documented in the Orkhon inscriptions — the oldest known Turkic texts

name: Uyghur Khaganate
note: One of history's few Manichaean states; adopted Manichaeism as state religion after 762 — a major civilizational break from Göktürk Tengriism

figures.name: Bayanchur Khan
figures.significance: Founded the Uyghur Khaganate; adopted Manichaeism as state religion

name: Khazar Khaganate
note: Ruling class converted to Judaism — a unique case; possibly a diplomatic choice to remain neutral between the Christian Byzantine and Muslim Abbasid spheres

figures.name: Bulan
figures.significance: The Khazar ruler who converted to Judaism

name: Mongol Empire
note: Religiously tolerant by policy (Yasa law); the different successor khanates each converted to the local dominant religion

policies.name: Yasa — Religious Tolerance Policy
policies.description: Mongol legal code mandated religious tolerance. Policy — each successor khanate converted to local religions independently.

figures.name: Genghis Khan
figures.significance: Greatest conqueror by territory in history; created the Yasa and the meritocratic military system

figures.name: Ogedei Khan
figures.significance: Completed the conquest of North China; launched the invasion of Europe

name: Golden Horde
note: Converted to Sunni Islam ~1313 under Özbeg Khan; Mongol rulers gradually Turkified — adopted Kipchak Turkic as the dominant language

figures.name: Batu Khan
figures.significance: Conquered Russia and Eastern Europe; founded the Golden Horde

figures.name: Ozbeg Khan
figures.significance: Converted the Golden Horde to Sunni Islam (1313); modern Uzbeks are named after him

name: Chagatai Khanate
note: Chagatai Turkic became the prestige literary language of Central Asia after Mongol Turkification

figures.name: Chagatai Khan
figures.significance: Second son of Genghis Khan; Chagatai Turkic language is named after him

name: Hunnic Empire
note: Attila's confederation devastated the Roman Empire and triggered the Great Migration. The Huns are of uncertain ethnic origin (possibly Turkic, possibly mixed). Their western empire collapsed immediately after Attila's death (453).

figures.name: Attila
figures.significance: Scourge of God; defeated Roman and Germanic armies; received tribute from both Eastern and Western Rome; his death at a wedding feast collapsed the empire overnight

name: Avar Khaganate
note: Avars (steppe nomads of uncertain Mongolian/Turkic origin) conquered the Carpathian basin in 568 after the Lombards left for Italy. They raided Byzantium and the Frankish kingdoms repeatedly. Charlemagne destroyed the Avar state in a series of campaigns (791-796) and dismantled their treasury ('the Ring').

figures.name: Bayan I
figures.significance: First great Avar Khagan; defeated the Frankish kingdom; established Avar dominance over the middle Danube basin

name: Kazakh Khanate
note: Emerged from the Uzbek (Shaybanid) breakup when Janybek and Giray led a nomadic confederation into the Kazakh steppe. The three zhuz (hordes: Great, Middle, Small) structure defined Kazakh society. Gradually subordinated by Russian expansion; the Small Zhuz accepted Russian protectorate in 1731 under Dzungar pressure.

figures.name: Janybek Khan
figures.significance: Co-founder of the Kazakh Khanate with Giray; established Kazakh identity as distinct from Uzbeks

figures.name: Ablai Khan
figures.significance: Greatest 18th-century Kazakh ruler; united the zhuz against the Dzungars; played Russia and China against each other

name: Crimean Khanate
note: Last surviving Mongol successor state on the Pontic steppe; Ottoman vassal from 1475. Conducted slave raids into Ukraine, Poland, and Russia — the Crimean slave trade depopulated vast areas of Eastern Europe. Peter the Great and Catherine the Great progressively curtailed Crimean power; annexed by Russia in 1783.

figures.name: Mengli Giray
figures.significance: First fully independent Khan; entered Ottoman vassalage; defeated the Grand Duchy of Lithuania and sacked Kyiv

figures.name: Devlet I Giray
figures.significance: Sacked Moscow in 1571 — the last time a nomadic force sacked the Russian capital; demanded tribute from Ivan the Terrible

name: North Yuan
note: After the Ming drove the Yuan from China (1368), the Mongol khans retreated to the steppe and continued to claim the title of Great Khan of the Mongol Empire. Periodic reunifications under strong khans (Dayan Khan, Altan Khan) challenged Ming China. Tibetan Buddhism progressively replaced shamanism/Tengriism. Formally ended when the Qing incorporated most Mongol tribes (1635-1691).

figures.name: Dayan Khan
figures.significance: Reunified the eastern Mongols after a century of fragmentation; reorganised the Mongol tribes into six tumens; his descendants ruled the Mongols for generations

figures.name: Altan Khan
figures.significance: Built Hohhot; established formal relations with Tibet — invited the Dalai Lama title and Tibetan Buddhism; the decisive conversion of Mongols to Buddhism
